records.ety: [OE. forgiven, foryiven, foryeven, AS. forgiefan, forgifan; perh. for- + giefan, gifan to give; cf. D. vergeven, G. vergeben, Icel. fyrirgefa, Sw. f<?/rgifva, Goth. fragiban to give, grant. See For-, and Give, v. t.]
records.morph: [imp. Forgave (?); p. p. Forgiven (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Forgiving]

defs: To give wholly; to make over without reservation; to resign.
quotes: To them that list the world's gay shows I leave, And to great ones such folly do forgive.

defs: To give up resentment or claim to requital on account of (an offense or wrong); to remit the penalty of; to pardon; -- said in reference to the act forgiven.
quotes: And their sins should be forgiven them.
He forgive injures so readily that he might be said to invite them.

defs: To cease to feel resentment against, on account of wrong committed; to give up claim to requital from or retribution upon (an offender); to absolve; to pardon; -- said of the person offending.
quotes: Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do.
I as free forgive you, as I would be fforgiven.
notes: ☞ Sometimes both the person and the offense follow as objects of the verb, sometimes one and sometimes the other being the indirect object. “Forgive us our debts as we forgive our debtors.” Matt. vi. 12. “Be of good cheer; thy sins be forgiven thee.” Matt. ix. 2.
